1	# Machine Config Operator (MCO) - Agent Entry Point
2	
3	## What This Component Does
4	
5	MCO manages node-level OS and configuration for OpenShift clusters. It owns the full lifecycle: generating Ignition configs from CRDs, rendering merged configs per pool, serving configs to bootstrapping nodes, and applying them on each node via a daemonset agent (MCD). It also supports on-cluster layering (OCL) where custom OS images are built from Containerfiles.
6	
7	## Repository Layout
8	
9	```
10	cmd/                          # 7 binaries (operator, controller, daemon, server, machine-os-builder, osimagestream, apiserver-watcher)
11	pkg/controller/               # All controllers (render, template, node, build, kubelet-config, container-runtime-config, bootimage, certrotation, pinnedimageset, drain, internalreleaseimage)
12	pkg/controller/common/        # Shared controller context, helpers, metrics, feature gates
13	pkg/daemon/                   # Machine Config Daemon - node agent that applies configs
14	pkg/server/                   # Machine Config Server - serves Ignition to bootstrapping nodes
15	pkg/operator/                 # Top-level operator sync, status, rendering
16	pkg/osimagestream/            # OS image stream provider and metadata
17	templates/                    # Ignition/systemd templates (common/, master/, worker/)
18	manifests/                    # Deployed manifests, RBAC, ValidatingAdmissionPolicies
19	test/                         # E2E suites (e2e-1of2, e2e-2of2, e2e-techpreview, e2e-bootstrap, e2e-ocl-*, e2e-single-node)
20	test/framework/               # envtest setup, client initialization
21	test/helpers/                 # Test builders, assertions, utilities
22	devex/cmd/                    # Developer tools (mcdiff, mco-push, wait-for-mcp, etc.)
23	hack/                         # Build, test, lint, template generation scripts
24	lib/                          # resourceapply, resourceread utilities
25	```
26	
27	## CRDs (API group: machineconfiguration.openshift.io/v1)
28	
29	| CRD | Purpose | Key Controller |
30	|-----|---------|----------------|
31	| MachineConfig | Ignition config fragment (files, units, kernel args, extensions) | Render |
32	| MachineConfigPool | Groups nodes by label selector, tracks update status | Node |
33	| ControllerConfig | Cluster-wide config (DNS, proxy, images, certs) | Template |
34	| KubeletConfig | Kubelet tuning applied per pool | KubeletConfig |
35	| ContainerRuntimeConfig | CRI-O/podman tuning per pool | ContainerRuntimeConfig |
36	| MachineConfigNode | Per-node update progress and state | Node/Daemon |
37	| MachineOSConfig | OCL build inputs (Containerfile, pool ref) | Build |
38	| MachineOSBuild | OCL build execution and status | Build |
39	| OSImageStream | OS image references per release | OSImageStream |
40	| PinnedImageSet | Pre-pulled container images per pool | PinnedImageSet |
41	
42	## Update Flow
43	
44	1. Template Controller renders MachineConfigs from ControllerConfig + templates
45	2. KubeletConfig/ContainerRuntimeConfig controllers generate additional MachineConfigs
46	3. Render Controller merges all MachineConfigs for each pool into a rendered config
47	4. Node Controller assigns target config to nodes, coordinates drain/reboot
48	5. MCD (daemon) on each node fetches and applies the config (Ignition, rpm-ostree/bootc, reboot)
49	6. For OCL: Build Controller creates Jobs to build layered OS images from MachineOSConfig
50	
51	## Key Development Commands
52	
53	```bash
54	make binaries                 # Build all components
55	make test-unit                # Unit tests (no cluster needed)
56	make bootstrap-e2e            # Bootstrap tests via envtest
57	make test-e2e                 # Full e2e (requires cluster)
58	make lint                     # golangci-lint
59	make verify                   # All verification checks
60	make update                   # Regenerate templates from vendor
61	make go-deps                  # go mod tidy + vendor
62	make helpers                  # Build devex tools
63	```
64	
65	## Component-Specific Documentation
66	
67	- [ai-docs/domain/](ai-docs/domain/) - CRD details and domain model
68	- [ai-docs/architecture/components.md](ai-docs/architecture/components.md) - Component internals
69	- [ai-docs/decisions/](ai-docs/decisions/) - Architecture decisions
70	- [ai-docs/references/ecosystem.md](ai-docs/references/ecosystem.md) - Platform doc links
71	- [MCO_DEVELOPMENT.md](MCO_DEVELOPMENT.md) - Development guide
72	- [MCO_TESTING.md](MCO_TESTING.md) - Testing guide
73	- [docs/HACKING.md](docs/HACKING.md) - Upstream development guide
74	
75	## Platform Documentation
76	
77	Generic OpenShift operator patterns, testing practices, and security guidelines live in the platform hub at `openshift/enhancements/ai-docs/`. This repo documents only MCO-specific knowledge.

1	# MCO Development Guide
2	
3	Component-specific development guidance. For generic OpenShift operator patterns, see the platform hub (`openshift/enhancements/ai-docs/`). For detailed upstream development instructions, see [docs/HACKING.md](docs/HACKING.md).
4	
5	## Prerequisites
6	
7	- Go 1.25.3+
8	- podman (for image builds)
9	- Access to an OpenShift cluster (for e2e tests)
10	- golangci-lint (installed automatically by `make lint`)
11	
12	## Building
13	
14	```bash
15	make binaries                          # All 8 binaries
16	make machine-config-operator           # Just the operator
17	make machine-config-daemon             # Just the daemon
18	make machine-config-controller         # Just the controller
19	make image                             # Container image via podman
20	make helpers                           # Developer tools (devex/cmd/)
21	```
22	
23	Build embeds version info from `git describe`, commit hash, and build date via `hack/build-go.sh`.
24	
25	## Dependencies
26	
27	```bash
28	make go-deps                           # go mod tidy + vendor + verify + chmod scripts
29	```
30	
31	Vendor directory is committed. All deps must be vendored. After updating `go.mod`, always run `make go-deps`.
32	
33	## Templates
34	
35	MCO generates MachineConfigs from Go templates in `templates/` (organized as `common/`, `master/`, `worker/`).
36	
37	```bash
38	make update                            # Regenerate templates from vendor sources
39	make verify-templates                  # Check templates are current
40	```
41	
42	Templates are generated from vendored systemd unit files. The Template Controller loads them from `/etc/mcc/templates` at runtime. When modifying templates, run `make update` followed by `make verify-templates`.
43	
44	## Code Generation
45	
46	CRD types are defined upstream in `github.com/openshift/api/machineconfiguration/v1/`. MCO vendors these types. To update:
47	
48	1. Update the `openshift/api` dependency in `go.mod`
49	2. Run `make go-deps`
50	3. Run `make update` if template sources changed
51	
52	Feature-gated CRD variants are generated upstream and vendored as `zz_generated.crd-manifests/`.
53	
54	## Controller Development
55	
56	All controllers live in `pkg/controller/`. Shared infrastructure is in `pkg/controller/common/`:
57	
58	- `controller_context.go` - Shared informer factories, client sets
59	- `helpers.go` - MC manipulation, Ignition conversion, annotation helpers
60	- `metrics.go` - Prometheus metric registration
61	- `featuregates.go` - Feature gate detection
62	
63	Controllers follow the standard pattern: watch resources via informers, enqueue on change, reconcile in `syncHandler`. Use `lib/resourceapply/` for idempotent create-or-update.
64	
65	## Daemon Development
66	
67	The MCD (`pkg/daemon/`) runs on every node. Key files:
68	
69	- `daemon.go` (~110KB) - Main daemon loop, state machine
70	- `update.go` (~135KB) - Update orchestration, reboot/reload decisions
71	- `config_drift_monitor.go` - Unauthorized change detection
72	- `rpm-ostree.go` / `bootc.go` - OS update backends
73	
74	To iterate on MCD without rebuilding the full image, see the "MCD Iteration" section in [docs/HACKING.md](docs/HACKING.md) which describes copying the binary directly to nodes.
75	
76	## Developer Tools
77	
78	Install with `make install-helpers`. Located in `devex/cmd/`:
79	
80	| Tool | Purpose |
81	|------|---------|
82	| `mcdiff` | Diff two MachineConfigs to understand changes |
83	| `mco-push` | Push local MCO binary to a live cluster for testing |
84	| `wait-for-mcp` | Block until a MachineConfigPool finishes updating |
85	| `mco-builder` | Build custom OS images |
86	| `mco-sanitize` | Prepare a cluster for testing |
87	| `onclustertesting` | Run tests on a live cluster |
88	| `run-on-all-nodes` | Execute a command on all cluster nodes |
89	
90	## Linting
91	
92	```bash
93	make lint                              # golangci-lint (10m timeout)
94	```
95	
96	Config in `.golangci.yml`. Supports `GOTAGS` for conditional compilation. CI produces checkstyle XML and JUnit output.
97	
98	## Verification
99	
100	```bash
101	make verify                            # All checks: lint, verify-e2e, verify-templates, verify-helpers
102	```
103	
104	Run before submitting PRs. CI runs these same checks.
105	
106	## AMI Updates
107	
108	```bash
109	make update-amis                       # Sync AMI mappings from openshift/installer
110	```
111	
112	Updates the AMI list in `pkg/controller/bootimage/ami.go` from the installer's region-to-AMI mappings.
113

1	# MCO Testing Guide
2	
3	Component-specific testing guidance. For generic OpenShift testing patterns, see the platform hub (`openshift/enhancements/ai-docs/`).
4	
5	## Test Suites Overview
6	
7	| Suite | Command | Cluster Required | Timeout | Description |
8	|-------|---------|-----------------|---------|-------------|
9	| Unit | `make test-unit` | No | - | Tests in `pkg/`, `cmd/`, `lib/`, `devex/`, `test/helpers/` |
10	| Bootstrap | `make bootstrap-e2e` | No (envtest) | - | Verifies bootstrap rendering matches controller rendering |
11	| E2E Full | `make test-e2e` | Yes | 190m | All e2e tests (2 shards, parallel) |
12	| E2E Shard 1 | `make test-e2e-1of2` | Yes | 100m | MCD, systemd, daemon tests |
13	| E2E Shard 2 | `make test-e2e-2of2` | Yes | 100m | Controller, containers, node OS, extensions |
14	| Tech Preview | `make test-e2e-techpreview` | Yes | 170m | Feature-gated functionality |
15	| Single Node | `make test-e2e-single-node` | Yes (SNO) | - | Single-node cluster scenarios |
16	| OCL Shard 1 | `make test-e2e-ocl-1of2` | Yes (OCL) | - | On-cluster layering tests (part 1) |
17	| OCL Shard 2 | `make test-e2e-ocl-2of2` | Yes (OCL) | - | On-cluster layering tests (part 2) |
18	| IRI | `make test-e2e-iri` | Yes | - | Internal Release Image runtime tests |
19	
20	## Unit Tests
21	
22	```bash
23	make test-unit
24	```
25	
26	Runs via `hack/test-unit.sh`. Covers all Go packages except e2e directories. Generates JUnit XML output when `ARTIFACT_DIR` is set. Coverage reports are produced when running in CI.
27	
28	Unit test files live alongside the code they test (standard Go convention).
29	
30	## Bootstrap Tests (envtest)
31	
32	```bash
33	make bootstrap-e2e
34	```
35	
36	Uses controller-runtime's envtest (real etcd + kube-apiserver, no full cluster). Verifies that:
37	- Bootstrap manifest parsing works correctly
38	- Bootstrap-rendered configs match what the controller would produce
39	- Pre-built image validation is correct
40	
41	Test data in `pkg/controller/bootstrap/testdata/bootstrap/`. Kubebuilder tools version: 1.32.1.
42	
43	The envtest setup installs MCO CRDs, OpenShift config CRDs, and operator CRDs into the test environment.
44	
45	## E2E Tests
46	
47	E2E tests require a running OpenShift cluster with MCO deployed. Tests are split into shards for CI parallelism.
48	
49	**Framework:** Ginkgo v2 + Gomega  
50	**Location:** `test/e2e-*of*/ directories`  
51	**Shared code:** `test/e2e-shared-tests/`, `test/helpers/`
52	
53	### Test Organization
54	
55	- `test/e2e-1of2/` - Daemon-focused: MCD apply, systemd units, file writes
56	- `test/e2e-2of2/` - Controller-focused: rendering, container runtime, node OS, extensions
57	- `test/e2e-techpreview/` - Feature-gated tests (require TechPreview cluster)
58	- `test/e2e-ocl-*of*/` - On-cluster layering tests (require OCL-enabled cluster)
59	
60	### Test Helpers (`test/helpers/`)
61	
62	- `assertions.go` - Custom Gomega matchers and assertion helpers
63	- `nodebuilder.go`, `poolbuilder.go` - Fluent builders for test objects
64	- `idempotent.go` - Idempotency verification utilities
65	- `archiveartifact.go` - Artifact collection for CI
66	- `emptyimage.go` - Creates empty container images for testing
67	
68	### Running E2E Tests Locally
69	
70	1. Ensure `KUBECONFIG` points to your cluster
71	2. Run desired suite: `make test-e2e-1of2`
72	3. Tests run sequentially (`-p 1`), fail-fast enabled
73	4. Artifacts collected to `ARTIFACT_DIR` if set
74	
75	## Verification (Compile Checks)
76	
77	```bash
78	make verify-e2e                        # Compile-check all e2e suites
79	make verify                            # All checks (lint + verify-e2e + verify-templates + verify-helpers)
80	```
81	
82	`verify-e2e` compiles but does not run e2e tests. Catches build errors without requiring a cluster.
83	
84	## CI Integration
85	
86	CI uses OpenShift CI (ci-operator). Config in `.ci-operator.yaml`.
87	
88	- Unit tests and linting run on every PR
89	- E2E tests run against provisioned clusters
90	- JUnit XML output via `hack/test-with-junit.sh`
91	- Artifacts collected to `ARTIFACT_DIR`
92	
93	## Writing New Tests
94	
95	**Unit tests:** Place `_test.go` files alongside the code. Use standard Go testing or testify.
96	
97	**E2E tests:** Add to the appropriate shard directory. Use the test framework:
98	```go
99	import (
100	    "github.com/openshift/machine-config-operator/test/helpers"
101	    "github.com/openshift/machine-config-operator/test/framework"
102	)
103	```
104	
105	Use builders from `test/helpers/` for creating test objects. Use `test/e2e-shared-tests/` for test logic shared across suites.
106	
107	**Bootstrap tests:** Add test cases to `test/e2e-bootstrap/` using envtest framework from `test/framework/envtest.go`.
108

1	# Custom pools
2	
3	Custom pools are pools that inherit from the `worker` pool. They use any MachineConfig targeted for the `worker` pool
4	but add the ability to deploy changes only targeted at the custom pool.
5	Since a custom pool inherits from the `worker` pool, any change to the `worker` pool is going to roll out to the custom pool as well (like an OS update during an upgrade).
6	
7	**Note:** Custom pools on master nodes are not supported by MCO. For more detail, see [Custom pool on master node](#Custom-pool-on-master-node)
8	
9	## Creating a custom pool
10	
11	The first thing to do to create a custom pool is labeling the node with a custom role - in our example it will be `infra`:
12	
13	```console
14	$ oc label node ip-10-0-130-218.us-west-1.compute.internal node-role.kubernetes.io/infra=
15	```
16	
17	```console
18	$ oc get nodes
19	NAME                                         STATUS    ROLES          AGE   VERSION
20	ip-10-0-130-218.us-west-1.compute.internal   Ready     infra,worker   37m   v1.14.0+e020ea5b3
21	ip-10-0-131-9.us-west-1.compute.internal     Ready     master         43m   v1.14.0+e020ea5b3
22	ip-10-0-134-237.us-west-1.compute.internal   Ready     master         43m   v1.14.0+e020ea5b3
23	ip-10-0-138-167.us-west-1.compute.internal   Ready     worker         37m   v1.14.0+e020ea5b3
24	ip-10-0-151-146.us-west-1.compute.internal   Ready     master         43m   v1.14.0+e020ea5b3
25	ip-10-0-152-59.us-west-1.compute.internal    Ready     worker         37m   v1.14.0+e020ea5b3
26	```
27	
28	When we create infra nodes, we apply them to existing worker nodes first. If you want to use the node as a purely infra node, you can remove the worker pool as follows:
29	
30	```
31	oc label node ip-10-0-130-218.us-west-1.compute.internal node-role.kubernetes.io/worker-
32	```
33	
34	This will not change the files on the node itself: the infra pool inherits from workers by default. This means that if you add a new MachineConfig to update workers, a purely infra node will still get updated. However this will mean that workloads scheduled for workers will no longer be scheduled on this node, as it no longer has the worker label.
35	
36	Next you need to create a MachineConfigPool that contains both the `worker` role and your custom one as MachineConfig selector as follows:
37	
38	```console
39	$ cat infra.mcp.yaml
40	apiVersion: machineconfiguration.openshift.io/v1
41	kind: MachineConfigPool
42	metadata:
43	  name: infra
44	spec:
45	  machineConfigSelector:
46	    matchExpressions:
47	      - {key: machineconfiguration.openshift.io/role, operator: In, values: [worker,infra]}
48	  nodeSelector:
49	    matchLabels:
50	      node-role.kubernetes.io/infra: ""
51	```
52	
53	```console
54	$ oc create -f infra.mcp.yaml
55	```
56	
57	You can check that the custom MCP has now been created:
58	
59	```console
60	$ oc get mcp
61	NAME     CONFIG                                             UPDATED   UPDATING   DEGRADED
62	infra    rendered-infra-6db67f47c0b205c26561b1c5ab74d79b    True      False      False
63	master   rendered-master-7053d8fc3619388accc12c7759f8241a   True      False      False
64	worker   rendered-worker-6db67f47c0b205c26561b1c5ab74d79b   True      False      False
65	```
66	
67	The example above makes an `infra` pool that contains all of the MachineConfigs used by the `worker` pool.
68	
69	## Deploy changes to a custom pool (optional)
70	
71	Deploying changes to a custom pool is just a matter of creating a MachineConfig that uses the custom pool name as the label (`infra` in the example):
72	
73	```console
74	$ cat infra.mc.yaml
75	apiVersion: machineconfiguration.openshift.io/v1
76	kind: MachineConfig
77	metadata:
78	  labels:
79	    machineconfiguration.openshift.io/role: infra
80	  name: 51-infra
81	spec:
82	  config:
83	    ignition:
84	      version: 2.2.0
85	    storage:
86	      files:
87	      - contents:
88	          source: data:,infra
89	        filesystem: root
90	        mode: 0644
91	        path: /etc/infratest
92	```
93	
94	```console
95	$ oc create -f infra.mc.yaml
96	```
97	
98	The custom `infra` pool has now deployed your change with the `infra`-targeted MachineConfig:
99	
100	```console
101	$ oc get mcp
102	NAME     CONFIG                                             UPDATED   UPDATING   DEGRADED
103	infra    rendered-infra-dfdfdf7e006f18cd5d29cae03f77948b    True      False      False
104	master   rendered-master-7053d8fc3619388accc12c7759f8241a   True      False      False
105	worker   rendered-worker-6db67f47c0b205c26561b1c5ab74d79b   True      False      False
106	```
107	
108	Now check that the file landed on the `infra` node:
109	
110	```console
111	$ oc get pods -n openshift-machine-config-operator -l k8s-app=machine-config-daemon --field-selector "spec.nodeName=ip-10-0-130-218.us-west-1.compute.internal"
112	NAME                          READY   STATUS    RESTARTS   AGE
113	machine-config-daemon-vxb4c   1/1     Running   2          43m
114	```
115	
116	```console
117	$ oc rsh -n openshift-machine-config-operator machine-config-daemon-vxb4c chroot /rootfs cat /etc/infratest
118	infra
119	```
120	
121	## Removing a custom pool
122	
123	Removing a custom pool requires first to un-label each node:
124	
125	```console
126	$ oc label node ip-10-0-130-218.us-west-1.compute.internal node-role.kubernetes.io/infra-
127	```
128	
129	Note: a node must have a role at any given time to be properly functioning. If you have infra-only nodes,
130	you should first relabel the node as worker and only then proceed to unlabel it from the infra role.
131	
132	```console
133	$ oc get nodes
134	NAME                                         STATUS   ROLES    AGE   VERSION
135	ip-10-0-130-218.us-west-1.compute.internal   Ready    worker   50m   v1.14.0+e020ea5b3
136	ip-10-0-131-9.us-west-1.compute.internal     Ready    master   56m   v1.14.0+e020ea5b3
137	ip-10-0-134-237.us-west-1.compute.internal   Ready    master   56m   v1.14.0+e020ea5b3
138	ip-10-0-138-167.us-west-1.compute.internal   Ready    worker   50m   v1.14.0+e020ea5b3
139	ip-10-0-151-146.us-west-1.compute.internal   Ready    master   56m   v1.14.0+e020ea5b3
140	ip-10-0-152-59.us-west-1.compute.internal    Ready    worker   50m   v1.14.0+e020ea5b3
141	```
142	
143	The MCO is then going to reconcile the node to the `worker` pool configuration.
144	
145	```console
146	$ oc get mcp
147	NAME     CONFIG                                             UPDATED   UPDATING   DEGRADED
148	infra    rendered-infra-dfdfdf7e006f18cd5d29cae03f77948b    True      False      False
149	master   rendered-master-7053d8fc3619388accc12c7759f8241a   True      False      False
150	worker   rendered-worker-6db67f47c0b205c26561b1c5ab74d79b   False     True       False
151	```
152	
153	As soon as the `worker` pool reconciles, you can remove the `infra` MCP and any MC created.
154	
155	```console
156	$ oc get mcp
157	NAME     CONFIG                                             UPDATED   UPDATING   DEGRADED
158	infra    rendered-infra-dfdfdf7e006f18cd5d29cae03f77948b    True      False      False
159	master   rendered-master-7053d8fc3619388accc12c7759f8241a   True      False      False
160	worker   rendered-worker-6db67f47c0b205c26561b1c5ab74d79b   True      False      False
161	
162	$ oc delete mc 51-infra
163	machineconfig.machineconfiguration.openshift.io "51-infra" deleted
164	
165	$ oc delete mcp infra
166	machineconfigpool.machineconfiguration.openshift.io "infra" deleted
167	```
168	
169	## Custom pool on master node
170	Custom pool on a node having master role is not supported. `oc label node` will apply the new custom role to the target master node but MCO will not apply changes specific to the custom pool. Error can be seen in the Machine Config Controller pod logs. This behaviour is to make sure that control plane nodes remain stable.
171	
172	## Understanding custom pool updates
173	
174	A node can be part of at most one pool.  The MCO will roll out updates for pools independently; for example, if there is an OS update or other change that affects all pools, normally 1 node from the `master` and `worker` pool would update at the same time.  If you add an `infra` pool for example, then 1 node from that pool will also try to roll out concurrently with the `master` and `worker`.
175

1	# Summary
2	
3	Users need a way to update Kubelet configuration and Kubelet tuneables. Users can do this via MCO currently, but they need to know the correct options to use. We can hopefully help customers by documenting a CRD with the Kubelet tuneables and have the MCO use these tuneables when rendering the kubelet.conf and kubelet.system files to Ignition/disk.
4	
5	Runtime configuration is configured via Kubelet as well. The CRI-O runtime is used by default and is the recommended solution. Some customers may have Docker specific tools or monitoring products they have paid for.
6	
7	# Motivation
8	
9	The MCO contains the logic to write the kubelet.conf configuration file and kubelet.system systemd unit file to Ignition. When Ignition starts on a machine it writes these two files to configure the kubelet. When these resources change within the MachineConfig, the new configs are rewritten and the MachineConfigDaemon is instructed to reboot the machine to pick up the new configs.
10	
11	## Goals
12	
13	The following goals should not require explicit knowledge of a customer to know the right command-line or configuration options to use within the Kubelet.
14	
15	1. changing out of resource handling ([https://docs.openshift.com/container-platform/3.11/admin_guide/out_of_resource_handling.html#out-of-resource-create-config](https://docs.openshift.com/container-platform/3.11/admin_guide/out_of_resource_handling.html#out-of-resource-create-config))
16	
17	2. setting a feature gate ([https://docs.openshift.com/container-platform/3.11/install_config/configuring_ephemeral.html#ephemeral-storage-enabling-ephemeral-storage](https://docs.openshift.com/container-platform/3.11/install_config/configuring_ephemeral.html#ephemeral-storage-enabling-ephemeral-storage))
18	
19	3. setting cpu manager to static ([https://docs.openshift.com/container-platform/3.11/scaling_performance/using_cpu_manager.html](https://docs.openshift.com/container-platform/3.11/scaling_performance/using_cpu_manager.html))
20	
21	4. setting max pods per node ([https://docs.openshift.com/container-platform/3.11/admin_guide/manage_nodes.html#admin-guide-max-pods-per-node](https://docs.openshift.com/container-platform/3.11/admin_guide/manage_nodes.html#admin-guide-max-pods-per-node))
22	
23	5. configuring garbage collection ([https://docs.openshift.com/container-platform/3.11/admin_guide/garbage_collection.html](https://docs.openshift.com/container-platform/3.11/admin_guide/garbage_collection.html))
24	
25	6. configuring node resources ([https://docs.openshift.com/container-platform/3.11/admin_guide/manage_nodes.html#configuring-node-resources](https://docs.openshift.com/container-platform/3.11/admin_guide/manage_nodes.html#configuring-node-resources))
26	
27	## Non-Goals
28	
29	# Proposal
30	
31	Extend the Machine Config Operator to include a KubetletConfig CRD and KubeletConfigController. By using a KubeletConfig CRD there is an implicit allowlist of allowed user controlled options. Upon deleting the KubeletConfig instance the default config is restored.
32	
33	## CRD
34	
35	```
36	apiVersion: apiextensions.k8s.io/v1
37	kind: CustomResourceDefinition
38	metadata:
39	  name: kubeletconfigs.machineconfiguration.openshift.io
40	spec:
41	  group: machineconfiguration.openshift.io
42	  versions:
43	    - name: v1
44	      served: true
45	      storage: true
46	  scope: Cluster
47	  names:
48	    plural: kubeletconfigs
49	    singular: kubeletconfig
50	    kind: KubeletConfig
51	```
52	
53	## SPEC
54	
55	```
56	MachineConfigPoolSelector *metav1.LabelSelector
57	Runtime:
58	  Name  string
59	  Endpoint string
60	KubeletConfig
61	  [KubeletConfigurationSpec](https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/release-1.11/pkg/kubelet/apis/kubeletconfig/v1beta1/types.go#L45)
62	```
63	
64	## VALIDATION
65	
66	It's important to note that, since the fields of the kubelet configuration are directly fetched from upstream the validation
67	of those values is handled directly by the kubelet. Please refer to the upstream version of the relavent kubernetes for the
68	valid values of these fields. Invalid values of the kubelet configuration fields may render cluster nodes unusable.
69	
70	## Example - Setting the Kubelet Log Level
71	This is what an example `kubelet config` CR looks like. Note: you must make sure to add a label under `matchLabels` in the KubeletConfig CR:
72	
73	```
74	apiVersion: machineconfiguration.openshift.io/v1
75	kind: KubeletConfig
76	metadata:
77	  name: set-max-pods
78	spec:
79	  logLevel: 5
80	  machineConfigPoolSelector:
81	    matchLabels:
82	      pools.operator.machineconfiguration.openshift.io/worker: ""
83	  kubeletConfig:
84	    maxPods: 100
85	```
86	
87	The logLevel attribute is optional and will default to level 2. Increasing the logLevel
88	uses more IO, CPU, and resources on all the machines within the pool.
89	
90	Save your `kubeletconfig` locally, for example as maxpods.yaml
91	
92	The label in the above example corresponds to the worker MachineConfigPool. By default the master/worker
93	MachineConfigPool has labels pools.operator.machineconfiguration.openshift.io/{worker|master}: "" in OCP 4.6 and later. If you have a custom pool, or have an earlier OCP version, you can instead create a label yourself as follows:
94	
95	```
96	apiVersion: machineconfiguration.openshift.io/v1
97	kind: KubeletConfig
98	metadata:
99	  name: set-max-pods
100	spec:
101	  logLevel: 5
102	  machineConfigPoolSelector:
103	    matchLabels:
104	      custom-kubelet: small-pods
105	  kubeletConfig:
106	    maxPods: 100
107	```
108	
109	To roll out the pods limit changes to all the worker nodes (can switch this to master for the master nodes), add the label that you created, here: `custom-kubelet: small-pods` under labels in the machineConfigPool config:
110	
111	```
112	oc edit machineconfigpool worker
113	```
114	
115	Snippet of the machineConfigPool config with the matching label added:
116	
117	```
118	apiVersion: machineconfiguration.openshift.io/v1
119	kind: MachineConfigPool
120	metadata:
121	  creationTimestamp: 2019-04-10T16:39:39Z
122	  generation: 1
123	  labels:
124	    custom-kubelet: small-pods
125	  name: worker
126	  ...
127	```
128	Now apply the `kubeletconfig` that you created:
129	
130	```
131	$ oc apply -f maxpods.yaml
132	kubeletconfig.machineconfiguration.openshift.io/set-max-pods created
133	```
134	
135	Double check that it was created:
136	
137	```
138	$ oc get kubeletconfig
139	NAME           AGE
140	set-max-pods   6s
141	```
142	
143	Check to ensure that a new 99-worker-XXX-kubelet is created and that a new rendered worker is created:
144	
145	```
146	$ oc get machineconfigs
147	NAME                                 GENERATEDBYCONTROLLER                      IGNITIONVERSION   AGE
148	...
149	99-worker-kubelet-managed            fc45f8b73b2fc61e567f2111181d3e802f2565d7   3.1.0             7s
150	...
151	rendered-worker-45678XYZ             fc45f8b73b2fc61e567f2111181d3e802f2565d7   3.1.0             2s
152	...
153	```
154	
155	The changes should now be rolled out to each node in the worker pool via that new rendered-worker machine config. You can verify by checking
156	that the latest rendered-worker machine-config has been rolled out to the pools successfully:
157	
158	```
159	$ oc get mcp
160	NAME     CONFIG                     UPDATED   UPDATING   DEGRADED   MACHINECOUNT   READYMACHINECOUNT   UPDATEDMACHINECOUNT   DEGRADEDMACHINECOUNT   AGE
161	...
162	worker   rendered-worker-45678XYZ   True      False      False      3              3                   3                     0                      5m
163	...
164	```
165	
166	## Example - Setting the optimal system reserved
167	You can also use `KubeletConfig` to set the optimal system reserved values for memory and cpu.
168	
169	```
170	apiVersion: machineconfiguration.openshift.io/v1
171	kind: KubeletConfig
172	metadata:
173	  name: dynamic-node
174	spec:
175	  autoSizingReserved: true
176	  machineConfigPoolSelector:
177	    matchLabels:
178	      pools.operator.machineconfiguration.openshift.io/worker: ""
179	```
180	
181	The `autoSizingReserved` attribute is optional and will default to the value `false`.
182	
183	Save your `KubeletConfig` locally say, kubelet-dynamic-node.yaml
184	
185	The label in the above example corresponds to the worker MachineConfigPool. Similar approach can be take to apply the `KubeletConfig` to the master or custom pool.
186	
187	## Implementation Details
188	
189	The KubeletConfigController would perform the following steps:
190	
191	1. Validate the user defined KubeletConfig
192	
193	2. Render the current MachineConfig (storage.files.contents[kubelet.conf]) into the KubeletConfiguration structure
194	
195	3. Load the KubeletConfig from the passed in Spec.KubeletConfig
196	
197	4. Use mergo to merge the two structures
198	
199	5. Serialize the KubeletConfig to json
200	
201	6. Create or Update an ignition /etc/kubernetes/kubelet.conf file within a 99-[role]-kubelet-managed MachineConfig
202	
203	After deletion of the KubeletConfig instance the config will be reverted to the original kubelet config.
204	
205	## Runtime Selection
206	
207	### Requirements
208	
209	First time installs will **always** install with CRI-O. After the initial install, the user will be able to switch from CRI-O to Docker, or from Docker to CRI-O if commanded to.
210	
211	Setting the options with the KubeletConfig CRD will cause the node to drain and reboot. Upon reboot, the machine will be reconfigured to use docker as the runtime via Machine Config Daemon and a rollout to each node.
212	
213	**_Note: Docker is only available in Bring-Your-Own-RHEL (User Provisioned Infrastructure) configurations._**
214	
215	TODO: Network Daemon?
216	
217	TODO: crictl configuration?
218	
219	### Runtime Partition Requirements
220	
221	There are specific partitioning schemes that are supported.
222	
223	1. Single Root Partition (/): Single partitioning scheme where everything is on a root partition
224	
225	2. Control Partition (/var): There is a root (/) partition for the filesystem, in addition to a separate /var disk or partition for runtime specific data. This scheme is useful in cases where the customer wants a small root partition or run the runtime via another disk (ie: SSD)
226	
227	3. Control and Log Partition (/var and /var/log): There is a root (/) partition for the filesystem, in addition to a separate /var and /log disk or partition. This scheme is useful in cases where the customer wants to protect from log files filling up the primary image or runtime disk.
228

1	# MCO Domain Model - CRDs
2	
3	All CRDs belong to API group `machineconfiguration.openshift.io/v1`. Types are defined in `vendor/github.com/openshift/api/machineconfiguration/v1/`. Compatibility Level 1 (stable within major release, min 12 months).
4	
5	## MachineConfig
6	
7	Cluster-scoped Ignition config fragment. No status subresource.
8	
9	**Spec:** `config` (Ignition RawExtension), `kernelArguments` ([]string), `extensions` ([]string), `fips` (bool), `kernelType` (default|realtime|64k-pages), `osImageURL`, `baseOSExtensionsContainerImage`.
10	
11	Multiple MachineConfigs are merged by the Render Controller into a single rendered config per pool. Merge order is deterministic (sorted by name). Conflicts in file paths use last-writer-wins.
12	
13	## MachineConfigPool (short: mcp)
14	
15	Groups nodes via `nodeSelector` and selects MachineConfigs via `machineConfigSelector`. Tracks update rollout status.
16	
17	**Key spec:** `paused` (bool), `maxUnavailable` (IntOrString), `pinnedImageSets` (max 100), `configuration` (target rendered config).
18	
19	**Key status:** `machineCount`, `updatedMachineCount`, `readyMachineCount`, `degradedMachineCount`, `conditions` (Updated, Updating, Degraded, NodeDegraded, RenderDegraded, Building, BuildSuccess, BuildFailed, etc.).
20	
21	**Validation rules:** machineCount >= updatedMachineCount, machineCount >= readyMachineCount, etc.
22	
23	Default pools: `master`, `worker`. Custom pools inherit from worker via `machineConfigSelector`.
24	
25	## ControllerConfig
26	
27	Cluster-wide configuration consumed by Template Controller to generate base MachineConfigs.
28	
29	**Key spec:** `clusterDNSIP`, `rootCAData`, `kubeAPIServerServingCAData`, `additionalTrustBundle`, `pullSecret`, `images` (map), `baseOSContainerImage`, `releaseImage`, `proxy`, `infra`, `dns`, `ipFamilies` (IPv4|IPv6|DualStack|DualStackIPv6Primary), `networkType`.
30	
31	**Status conditions:** TemplateControllerRunning, TemplateControllerCompleted, TemplateControllerFailing.
32	
33	## KubeletConfig
34	
35	Applies upstream kubelet configuration overrides per pool.
36	
37	**Key spec:** `machineConfigPoolSelector` (LabelSelector), `kubeletConfig` (RawExtension - upstream KubeletConfiguration), `autoSizingReserved` (bool), `logLevel` (0-10), `tlsSecurityProfile`.
38	
39	Controller generates a MachineConfig with the merged kubelet config and drops file at `/etc/kubernetes/kubelet.conf`.
40	
41	## ContainerRuntimeConfig (short: ctrcfg)
42	
43	Tunes CRI-O and container runtime settings per pool.
44	
45	**Key spec:** `machineConfigPoolSelector`, `containerRuntimeConfig` with fields: `pidsLimit`, `logLevel` (fatal..debug), `logSizeMax` (>= 8192), `overlaySize` (default 10GB), `defaultRuntime` (crun|runc), `additionalLayerStores` (max 5), `additionalImageStores` (max 10), `additionalArtifactStores` (max 10).
46	
47	Store paths validated: absolute, 1-256 chars, pattern `^/[a-zA-Z0-9/._-]+$`, no consecutive slashes.
48	
49	## MachineConfigNode
50	
51	Per-node update tracking. Created/managed by Node Controller and updated by MCD.
52	
53	**Spec:** `configVersion.desired` (target MC ref), `pool.name`, `node.name`.
54	
55	**Status conditions:** Updated, UpdatePrepared, UpdateExecuted, UpdatePostActionComplete, UpdateComplete, Resumed, UpdateCompatible, AppliedFiles, AppliedFilesAndOS, Cordoned, Uncordoned, Drained, RebootedNode, ReloadedCRIO, PinnedImageSetsDegraded, PinnedImageSetsProgressing.
56	
57	## MachineOSConfig
58	
59	Defines on-cluster layering (OCL) build inputs for a pool.
60	
61	**Key spec:** `machineConfigPool` (ref), `buildInputs`, `containerFile` ([]ContainerFile with `content` and optional `containerfileArch`).
62	
63	## MachineOSBuild
64	
65	Tracks execution of an OCL build job.
66	
67	**Key spec:** `machineOSConfig` (ref), `machineConfig` (ref).
68	
69	**Key status:** `digestedImagePushSpec` (built image), `builder.job` (Job ref with name/namespace).
70	
71	## OSImageStream
72	
73	OS image references for the release. Managed by the osimagestream binary.
74	
75	**Status:** `defaultStream`, `availableStreams` ([]OSImageStreamSpec with `name`, `osImage`, `osExtensionsImage`).
76	
77	## PinnedImageSet
78	
79	Pre-pulls container images to nodes in matching pools.
80	
81	**Spec:** `machineConfigPoolSelector` (LabelSelector), `pinnedImages` ([]PinnedImage with `name`).
82	
83	## Feature-Gated CRD Variants
84	
85	CRDs ship in multiple variants for feature gates: Default, DevPreviewNoUpgrade, CustomNoUpgrade, TechPreviewNoUpgrade, OKD, Hypershift, SelfManagedHA. Located in `vendor/github.com/openshift/api/machineconfiguration/v1/zz_generated.crd-manifests/`.
86

1	# MCO Architecture - Component Internals
2	
3	## Binaries
4	
5	MCO produces 7 binaries from `cmd/`:
6	
7	| Binary | Deployment | Purpose |
8	|--------|-----------|---------|
9	| `machine-config-operator` | Deployment | Top-level operator: syncs all sub-components, manages ClusterOperator status |
10	| `machine-config-controller` | Deployment | Runs render, template, node, kubelet-config, container-runtime-config, certrotation, drain controllers |
11	| `machine-config-daemon` | DaemonSet | Node agent: fetches and applies configs, handles reboot/drain |
12	| `machine-config-server` | DaemonSet (masters) | Serves Ignition configs to bootstrapping/joining nodes via HTTPS |
13	| `machine-os-builder` | Deployment | Orchestrates OCL builds (MachineOSBuild jobs) |
14	| `machine-config-osimagestream` | Job | Generates OSImageStream from release payload |
15	| `apiserver-watcher` | DaemonSet | Monitors local apiserver, writes cloud-routes downfiles |
16	
17	## Controller Details
18	
19	### Template Controller (`pkg/controller/template/`)
20	- **Watches:** ControllerConfig, APIServer, Secrets, ConfigMaps
21	- **Produces:** Base MachineConfig objects from Go templates in `/etc/mcc/templates`
22	- **Templates organized by role:** `common/`, `master/`, `worker/`
23	- Merges IRI (Internal Release Image) secrets into generated configs
24	- Template rendering uses Go `text/template` with cluster state as input
25	
26	### Render Controller (`pkg/controller/render/`)
27	- **Watches:** MachineConfig, MachineConfigPool, OSImageStream, ControllerConfig, ContainerRuntimeConfig
28	- **Produces:** Rendered (merged) MachineConfig per pool
29	- Merge is deterministic: MachineConfigs sorted by name, last-writer-wins for file conflicts
30	- Uses content-based hashing for rendered config naming (avoids unnecessary updates)
31	- 5-second render delay to batch rapid changes
32	
33	### Node Controller (`pkg/controller/node/`)
34	- **Watches:** MachineConfigPool, MachineConfig, MachineOSConfig, MachineOSBuild, Node
35	- **Manages:** Node annotations (desired/current config), drain coordination, MachineConfigNode status
36	- Largest controller (~81KB). Orchestrates the full node update lifecycle:
37	  1. Detects config drift between desired and current
38	  2. Respects `maxUnavailable` for rolling updates
39	  3. Coordinates with MCD for drain, apply, reboot
40	  4. Updates MachineConfigPool status counters
41	
42	### Build Controller (`pkg/controller/build/`)
43	- **Watches:** MachineOSBuild, MachineOSConfig, Job, MachineConfigPool
44	- **Manages:** OCL build lifecycle via Kubernetes Jobs
45	- Subdirectories: `buildrequest/` (build job creation), `imagebuilder/` (podman/buildah), `imagepruner/` (cleanup)
46	- Creates Jobs that run `podman build` with user-provided Containerfiles
47	- Tracks build status through MachineOSBuild conditions
48	
49	### KubeletConfig Controller (`pkg/controller/kubelet-config/`)
50	- **Watches:** KubeletConfig, MachineConfigPool, ClusterOperator
51	- Merges upstream KubeletConfiguration into MachineConfig objects
52	- Handles feature gate propagation to kubelet config
53	- Supports `autoSizingReserved` for automatic resource reservation
54	
55	### ContainerRuntimeConfig Controller (`pkg/controller/container-runtime-config/`)
56	- **Watches:** ContainerRuntimeConfig, MachineConfigPool
57	- Generates CRI-O config files (`/etc/crio/crio.conf.d/`), storage config, registries config
58	- Large helper surface (~63KB helpers.go) for registry mirror configuration
59	
60	### Boot Image Controller (`pkg/controller/bootimage/`)
61	- **Watches:** MachineSet, ControlPlaneMachineSet, Infrastructure, ClusterVersion
62	- Updates cloud provider boot images (AMIs, Azure images, GCP images)
63	- Platform-specific logic for AWS, Azure, GCP, vSphere, OpenStack
64	- AMI mapping in `ami.go` (228KB of region-to-AMI mappings)
65	
66	### Other Controllers
67	- **CertRotation** (`pkg/controller/certrotation/`): Rotates MCO serving certificates
68	- **PinnedImageSet** (`pkg/controller/pinnedimageset/`): Ensures images are pre-pulled to nodes
69	- **Drain** (`pkg/controller/drain/`): Coordinates node drain operations
70	- **InternalReleaseImage** (`pkg/controller/internalreleaseimage/`): Extracts image refs from release payloads
71	
72	## Machine Config Daemon (`pkg/daemon/`)
73	
74	The node-level agent. Core file `daemon.go` is ~110KB, `update.go` is ~135KB.
75	
76	**Responsibilities:**
77	- Watches node annotations for desired config changes
78	- Applies Ignition configs: writes files, creates systemd units, sets kernel arguments
79	- Manages OS updates via rpm-ostree (traditional) or bootc (container-native)
80	- Decides reboot vs. service-reload based on change type
81	- Config drift monitoring (detects unauthorized file changes)
82	- Reports progress via MachineConfigNode conditions
83	- Pinned image set management (pre-pulling images)
84	- On-disk validation of applied configs
85	
86	**Update decision tree:**
87	- File-only changes with no OS change: service reload (CRIO, kubelet)
88	- OS image change or kernel args: full node reboot
89	- FIPS change: reboot required
90	- Extension changes: reboot required
91	
92	## Machine Config Server (`pkg/server/`)
93	
94	HTTPS server on master nodes serving Ignition configs to joining nodes.
95	
96	- `AppendersBuilder` pattern composes config from multiple sources
97	- Appenders: KubeConfig, certificates, node annotations
98	- Bootstrap mode: serves from filesystem during cluster install
99	- Cluster mode: serves from rendered MachineConfigs via API
100	
101	## Operator Sync (`pkg/operator/`)
102	
103	Top-level orchestration in `sync.go` (~113KB):
104	
105	1. Syncs OSImageStream (image references)
106	2. Renders and deploys controller, daemon, server components
107	3. Manages RBAC, ServiceAccounts, ConfigMaps, Secrets
108	4. Reports ClusterOperator status and conditions
109	5. Handles upgrades: version skew, feature gate transitions
110	
111	## Shared Infrastructure
112	
113	### `pkg/controller/common/`
114	- `controller_context.go`: Shared informer factory, client sets, feature gate state
115	- `helpers.go` (~56KB): MC manipulation, Ignition utilities, annotation helpers
116	- `layered_node_state.go`: State tracking for OCL nodes
117	- `metrics.go`: Prometheus metric definitions
118	- `featuregates.go`: Feature gate detection and propagation
119	
120	### `lib/resourceapply/` and `lib/resourceread/`
121	- Idempotent resource application (create-or-update pattern)
122	- Type-safe resource deserialization from YAML/JSON
123	
124	## ValidatingAdmissionPolicies
125	
126	Located in `manifests/`:
127	- `custom-machine-config-pool-selector`: Custom MCPs must inherit from worker
128	- `internalreleaseimage-deletion-guard`: Protects InternalReleaseImage from deletion
129	- `machineconfigpool-osimagestream-reference`: OSImageStream ref validation
130	- `machineconfiguration-guards`: Global MachineConfiguration guards
131	- `mcn-guards`: MachineConfigNode guards
132	- `osimagestream-deletion-guard`: Protects OSImageStream from deletion
133	- `update-bootimages`: Boot image update validation
134

1	# ADR-002: Deterministic Config Rendering
2	
3	## Status
4	Active
5	
6	## Context
7	Multiple MachineConfigs can target a single MachineConfigPool. The system needs a predictable, reproducible way to merge them into one rendered config.
8	
9	## Decision
10	The Render Controller sorts MachineConfigs by name and merges them sequentially. For file conflicts (same path in multiple MCs), last-writer-wins based on sort order. The rendered config name includes a content hash to avoid unnecessary rollouts when the effective config hasn't changed.
11	
12	A 5-second render delay batches rapid changes (e.g., multiple MCs created simultaneously) to avoid churn.
13	
14	## Consequences
15	- MC naming matters: alphabetically later MCs override earlier ones for conflicting files
16	- Content-based hashing means no-op updates don't trigger node rollouts
17	- The render delay means changes are not immediately visible (by design)
18	- Render failures set the `RenderDegraded` condition on the pool
19

10	    matchLabels:
11	      "machineconfiguration.openshift.io/role": "worker"
12	  nodeSelector:
13	    matchLabels:

1	# ADR-001: On-Cluster Layering (OCL)
2	
3	## Status
4	Implemented (TechPreview)
5	
6	## Context
7	Traditional MCO updates apply Ignition configs and use rpm-ostree to manage the OS. Users need to add custom packages or content to RHCOS nodes but have no supported mechanism to build custom OS images integrated with the MCO update lifecycle.
8	
9	## Decision
10	Introduce On-Cluster Layering: users create a `MachineOSConfig` with a Containerfile, MCO builds a custom OS image on-cluster via `MachineOSBuild` Jobs (podman build), and the resulting image is applied to nodes in the target pool.
11	
12	## Components
13	- `MachineOSConfig` CRD: build inputs (Containerfile, pool ref)
14	- `MachineOSBuild` CRD: build execution tracking
15	- `machine-os-builder` binary: orchestrates builds
16	- Build Controller (`pkg/controller/build/`): reconciles MachineOSBuild lifecycle
17	- Image Builder (`pkg/controller/build/imagebuilder/`): podman/buildah job creation
18	- Image Pruner (`pkg/controller/build/imagepruner/`): cleanup of old images
19	
20	## Consequences
21	- Node Controller must coordinate with build status before rolling updates
22	- MachineConfigPool gains build-related conditions (BuildPending, Building, BuildSuccess, BuildFailed)
23	- Daemon must support bootc-based updates in addition to rpm-ostree
24	- Feature-gated CRD variants needed for gradual rollout
25

2	kind: Template
3	metadata:
4	  name: mcp-template
5	objects:
6	  - kind: MachineConfigPool
7	    apiVersion: machineconfiguration.openshift.io/v1
8	    metadata:
9	      name: "${NAME}"
10	      labels:
11	        pools.operator.machineconfiguration.openshift.io/${NAME}: ""
12	    spec:
13	      machineConfigSelector:
14	        matchExpressions:
15	          - { key: machineconfiguration.openshift.io/role, operator: In, values: [ worker,"${NAME}" ] }
16	      nodeSelector:
17	        matchLabels:
18	          node-role.kubernetes.io/${NAME}: ""
19	parameters:
20	  - name: NAME
21

2	kind: Template

5	objects:

8	    metadata:

10	    spec:

12	        matchLabels:
13	          pools.operator.machineconfiguration.openshift.io/${POOL}: ''

16	  - name: NAME
17	  - name: KUBELETCONFIG
18	    value: "{}"

1	# ADR-003: Node Update Lifecycle
2	
3	## Status
4	Active
5	
6	## Context
7	Applying config changes to nodes requires coordination between the cluster-level controller and the node-level daemon to avoid disrupting workloads.
8	
9	## Decision
10	Updates follow a multi-phase lifecycle tracked by MachineConfigNode conditions:
11	
12	1. **Node Controller** detects config drift, respects `maxUnavailable`, sets target annotation
13	2. **MCD** picks up annotation change, cordons the node
14	3. **MCD** drains the node (evicts pods)
15	4. **MCD** applies config (files, systemd units, kernel args)
16	5. **MCD** performs OS update (rpm-ostree/bootc) if needed
17	6. **MCD** decides: reboot (OS/kernel changes) or service-reload (file-only changes)
18	7. **MCD** uncordons the node after successful apply
19	8. **Node Controller** updates pool status counters
20	
21	The daemon reports granular progress via MachineConfigNode conditions (AppliedFiles, AppliedFilesAndOS, Drained, Cordoned, RebootedNode, ReloadedCRIO, etc.).
22	
23	## Consequences
24	- `maxUnavailable` on MachineConfigPool controls rollout speed
25	- Degraded nodes block pool progress (require manual intervention)
26	- Config drift monitoring detects unauthorized changes between updates
27	- Pausing a pool (`paused: true`) halts all updates to that pool
28

24	| `openshift/client-go` | Generated clients for MCO CRDs |
25	| `openshift/library-go` | Operator scaffolding, resource apply patterns |
26	| `openshift/cluster-version-operator` | Manages MCO operator deployment, coordinates upgrades |
27	| `kubernetes/kubernetes` | Kubelet config, node API, drain utilities |
28	| `coreos/ignition` | Config format for node provisioning |
29	| `coreos/rpmostree-client-go` | Go client for rpm-ostree operations on nodes |
30	
31	## CI and Testing
32	
33	- **CI system:** OpenShift CI (ci-operator), config in `.ci-operator.yaml`
34	- **Test framework:** controller-runtime envtest (real etcd + kube-apiserver)
35	- **E2E framework:** Ginkgo v2 + Gomega
36	- **Kubebuilder tools version:** 1.32.1 (for envtest)
37	
38	## Internal Tools
39	
40	Developer experience tools in `devex/cmd/`:
41	- `mcdiff` - Compare MachineConfigs
42	- `mco-push` - Push MCO changes to a live cluster
43	- `mco-builder` - Build OS container images
44	- `wait-for-mcp` - Wait for MachineConfigPool to finish updating
45	- `onclustertesting` - Run tests against a live cluster
46	- `run-on-all-nodes` - Execute commands across all cluster nodes
47

1	# Summary
2	
3	Users need a way to update Kubelet configuration and Kubelet tuneables. Users can do this via MCO currently, but they need to know the correct options to use. We can hopefully help customers by documenting a CRD with the Kubelet tuneables and have the MCO use these tuneables when rendering the kubelet.conf and kubelet.system files to Ignition/disk.
4	
5	Runtime configuration is configured via Kubelet as well. The CRI-O runtime is used by default and is the recommended solution. Some customers may have Docker specific tools or monitoring products they have paid for.
6	
7	# Motivation
8	
9	The MCO contains the logic to write the kubelet.conf configuration file and kubelet.system systemd unit file to Ignition. When Ignition starts on a machine it writes these two files to configure the kubelet. When these resources change within the MachineConfig, the new configs are rewritten and the MachineConfigDaemon is instructed to reboot the machine to pick up the new configs.
10	
11	## Goals
12	
13	The following goals should not require explicit knowledge of a customer to know the right command-line or configuration options to use within the Kubelet.
14	
15	1. changing out of resource handling ([https://docs.openshift.com/container-platform/3.11/admin_guide/out_of_resource_handling.html#out-of-resource-create-config](https://docs.openshift.com/container-platform/3.11/admin_guide/out_of_resource_handling.html#out-of-resource-create-config))
16	
17	2. setting a feature gate ([https://docs.openshift.com/container-platform/3.11/install_config/configuring_ephemeral.html#ephemeral-storage-enabling-ephemeral-storage](https://docs.openshift.com/container-platform/3.11/install_config/configuring_ephemeral.html#ephemeral-storage-enabling-ephemeral-storage))
18	
19	3. setting cpu manager to static ([https://docs.openshift.com/container-platform/3.11/scaling_performance/using_cpu_manager.html](https://docs.openshift.com/container-platform/3.11/scaling_performance/using_cpu_manager.html))
20	
21	4. setting max pods per node ([https://docs.openshift.com/container-platform/3.11/admin_guide/manage_nodes.html#admin-guide-max-pods-per-node](https://docs.openshift.com/container-platform/3.11/admin_guide/manage_nodes.html#admin-guide-max-pods-per-node))
22	
23	5. configuring garbage collection ([https://docs.openshift.com/container-platform/3.11/admin_guide/garbage_collection.html](https://docs.openshift.com/container-platform/3.11/admin_guide/garbage_collection.html))
24	
25	6. configuring node resources ([https://docs.openshift.com/container-platform/3.11/admin_guide/manage_nodes.html#configuring-node-resources](https://docs.openshift.com/container-platform/3.11/admin_guide/manage_nodes.html#configuring-node-resources))
26	
27	## Non-Goals
28	
29	# Proposal
30	
31	Extend the Machine Config Operator to include a KubetletConfig CRD and KubeletConfigController. By using a KubeletConfig CRD there is an implicit allowlist of allowed user controlled options. Upon deleting the KubeletConfig instance the default config is restored.
32	
33	## CRD
34	
35	```
36	apiVersion: apiextensions.k8s.io/v1
37	kind: CustomResourceDefinition
38	metadata:
39	  name: kubeletconfigs.machineconfiguration.openshift.io
40	spec:
41	  group: machineconfiguration.openshift.io
42	  versions:
43	    - name: v1
44	      served: true
45	      storage: true
46	  scope: Cluster
47	  names:
48	    plural: kubeletconfigs
49	    singular: kubeletconfig
50	    kind: KubeletConfig
51	```
52	
53	## SPEC
54	
55	```
56	MachineConfigPoolSelector *metav1.LabelSelector
57	Runtime:
58	  Name  string
59	  Endpoint string
60	KubeletConfig
61	  [KubeletConfigurationSpec](https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/release-1.11/pkg/kubelet/apis/kubeletconfig/v1beta1/types.go#L45)
62	```
63	
64	## VALIDATION
65	
66	It's important to note that, since the fields of the kubelet configuration are directly fetched from upstream the validation
67	of those values is handled directly by the kubelet. Please refer to the upstream version of the relavent kubernetes for the
68	valid values of these fields. Invalid values of the kubelet configuration fields may render cluster nodes unusable.
69	
70	## Example - Setting the Kubelet Log Level
71	This is what an example `kubelet config` CR looks like. Note: you must make sure to add a label under `matchLabels` in the KubeletConfig CR:
72	
73	```
74	apiVersion: machineconfiguration.openshift.io/v1
75	kind: KubeletConfig
76	metadata:
77	  name: set-max-pods
78	spec:
79	  logLevel: 5
80	  machineConfigPoolSelector:
81	    matchLabels:
82	      pools.operator.machineconfiguration.openshift.io/worker: ""
83	  kubeletConfig:
84	    maxPods: 100
85	```
86	
87	The logLevel attribute is optional and will default to level 2. Increasing the logLevel
88	uses more IO, CPU, and resources on all the machines within the pool.
89	
90	Save your `kubeletconfig` locally, for example as maxpods.yaml
91	
92	The label in the above example corresponds to the worker MachineConfigPool. By default the master/worker
93	MachineConfigPool has labels pools.operator.machineconfiguration.openshift.io/{worker|master}: "" in OCP 4.6 and later. If you have a custom pool, or have an earlier OCP version, you can instead create a label yourself as follows:
94	
95	```
96	apiVersion: machineconfiguration.openshift.io/v1
97	kind: KubeletConfig
98	metadata:
99	  name: set-max-pods
100	spec:
101	  logLevel: 5
102	  machineConfigPoolSelector:
103	    matchLabels:
104	      custom-kubelet: small-pods
105	  kubeletConfig:
106	    maxPods: 100
107	```
108	
109	To roll out the pods limit changes to all the worker nodes (can switch this to master for the master nodes), add the label that you created, here: `custom-kubelet: small-pods` under labels in the machineConfigPool config:
110	
111	```
112	oc edit machineconfigpool worker
113	```
114	
115	Snippet of the machineConfigPool config with the matching label added:
116	
117	```
118	apiVersion: machineconfiguration.openshift.io/v1
119	kind: MachineConfigPool
120	metadata:
121	  creationTimestamp: 2019-04-10T16:39:39Z
122	  generation: 1
123	  labels:
124	    custom-kubelet: small-pods
125	  name: worker
126	  ...
127	```
128	Now apply the `kubeletconfig` that you created:
129	
130	```
131	$ oc apply -f maxpods.yaml
132	kubeletconfig.machineconfiguration.openshift.io/set-max-pods created
133	```
134	
135	Double check that it was created:
136	
137	```
138	$ oc get kubeletconfig
139	NAME           AGE
140	set-max-pods   6s
141	```
142	
143	Check to ensure that a new 99-worker-XXX-kubelet is created and that a new rendered worker is created:
144	
145	```
146	$ oc get machineconfigs
147	NAME                                 GENERATEDBYCONTROLLER                      IGNITIONVERSION   AGE
148	...
149	99-worker-kubelet-managed            fc45f8b73b2fc61e567f2111181d3e802f2565d7   3.1.0             7s
150	...
151	rendered-worker-45678XYZ             fc45f8b73b2fc61e567f2111181d3e802f2565d7   3.1.0             2s
152	...
153	```
154	
155	The changes should now be rolled out to each node in the worker pool via that new rendered-worker machine config. You can verify by checking
156	that the latest rendered-worker machine-config has been rolled out to the pools successfully:
157	
158	```
159	$ oc get mcp
160	NAME     CONFIG                     UPDATED   UPDATING   DEGRADED   MACHINECOUNT   READYMACHINECOUNT   UPDATEDMACHINECOUNT   DEGRADEDMACHINECOUNT   AGE
161	...
162	worker   rendered-worker-45678XYZ   True      False      False      3              3                   3                     0                      5m
163	...
164	```
165	
166	## Example - Setting the optimal system reserved
167	You can also use `KubeletConfig` to set the optimal system reserved values for memory and cpu.
168	
169	```
170	apiVersion: machineconfiguration.openshift.io/v1
171	kind: KubeletConfig
172	metadata:
173	  name: dynamic-node
174	spec:
175	  autoSizingReserved: true
176	  machineConfigPoolSelector:
177	    matchLabels:
178	      pools.operator.machineconfiguration.openshift.io/worker: ""
179	```
180	
181	The `autoSizingReserved` attribute is optional and will default to the value `false`.
182	
183	Save your `KubeletConfig` locally say, kubelet-dynamic-node.yaml
184	
185	The label in the above example corresponds to the worker MachineConfigPool. Similar approach can be take to apply the `KubeletConfig` to the master or custom pool.
186	
187	## Implementation Details
188	
189	The KubeletConfigController would perform the following steps:
190	
191	1. Validate the user defined KubeletConfig
192	
193	2. Render the current MachineConfig (storage.files.contents[kubelet.conf]) into the KubeletConfiguration structure
194	
195	3. Load the KubeletConfig from the passed in Spec.KubeletConfig
196	
197	4. Use mergo to merge the two structures
198	
199	5. Serialize the KubeletConfig to json
200	
201	6. Create or Update an ignition /etc/kubernetes/kubelet.conf file within a 99-[role]-kubelet-managed MachineConfig
202	
203	After deletion of the KubeletConfig instance the config will be reverted to the original kubelet config.
204	
205	## Runtime Selection
206	
207	### Requirements
208	
209	First time installs will **always** install with CRI-O. After the initial install, the user will be able to switch from CRI-O to Docker, or from Docker to CRI-O if commanded to.
210	
211	Setting the options with the KubeletConfig CRD will cause the node to drain and reboot. Upon reboot, the machine will be reconfigured to use docker as the runtime via Machine Config Daemon and a rollout to each node.
212	
213	**_Note: Docker is only available in Bring-Your-Own-RHEL (User Provisioned Infrastructure) configurations._**
214	
215	TODO: Network Daemon?
216	
217	TODO: crictl configuration?
218	
219	### Runtime Partition Requirements
220	
221	There are specific partitioning schemes that are supported.
222	
223	1. Single Root Partition (/): Single partitioning scheme where everything is on a root partition
224	
225	2. Control Partition (/var): There is a root (/) partition for the filesystem, in addition to a separate /var disk or partition for runtime specific data. This scheme is useful in cases where the customer wants a small root partition or run the runtime via another disk (ie: SSD)
226	
227	3. Control and Log Partition (/var and /var/log): There is a root (/) partition for the filesystem, in addition to a separate /var and /log disk or partition. This scheme is useful in cases where the customer wants to protect from log files filling up the primary image or runtime disk.
228

1	# Custom pools
2	
3	Custom pools are pools that inherit from the `worker` pool. They use any MachineConfig targeted for the `worker` pool
4	but add the ability to deploy changes only targeted at the custom pool.
5	Since a custom pool inherits from the `worker` pool, any change to the `worker` pool is going to roll out to the custom pool as well (like an OS update during an upgrade).
6	
7	**Note:** Custom pools on master nodes are not supported by MCO. For more detail, see [Custom pool on master node](#Custom-pool-on-master-node)
8	
9	## Creating a custom pool
10	
11	The first thing to do to create a custom pool is labeling the node with a custom role - in our example it will be `infra`:
12	
13	```console
14	$ oc label node ip-10-0-130-218.us-west-1.compute.internal node-role.kubernetes.io/infra=
15	```
16	
17	```console
18	$ oc get nodes
19	NAME                                         STATUS    ROLES          AGE   VERSION
20	ip-10-0-130-218.us-west-1.compute.internal   Ready     infra,worker   37m   v1.14.0+e020ea5b3
21	ip-10-0-131-9.us-west-1.compute.internal     Ready     master         43m   v1.14.0+e020ea5b3
22	ip-10-0-134-237.us-west-1.compute.internal   Ready     master         43m   v1.14.0+e020ea5b3
23	ip-10-0-138-167.us-west-1.compute.internal   Ready     worker         37m   v1.14.0+e020ea5b3
24	ip-10-0-151-146.us-west-1.compute.internal   Ready     master         43m   v1.14.0+e020ea5b3
25	ip-10-0-152-59.us-west-1.compute.internal    Ready     worker         37m   v1.14.0+e020ea5b3
26	```
27	
28	When we create infra nodes, we apply them to existing worker nodes first. If you want to use the node as a purely infra node, you can remove the worker pool as follows:
29	
30	```
31	oc label node ip-10-0-130-218.us-west-1.compute.internal node-role.kubernetes.io/worker-
32	```
33	
34	This will not change the files on the node itself: the infra pool inherits from workers by default. This means that if you add a new MachineConfig to update workers, a purely infra node will still get updated. However this will mean that workloads scheduled for workers will no longer be scheduled on this node, as it no longer has the worker label.
35	
36	Next you need to create a MachineConfigPool that contains both the `worker` role and your custom one as MachineConfig selector as follows:
37	
38	```console
39	$ cat infra.mcp.yaml
40	apiVersion: machineconfiguration.openshift.io/v1
41	kind: MachineConfigPool
42	metadata:
43	  name: infra
44	spec:
45	  machineConfigSelector:
46	    matchExpressions:
47	      - {key: machineconfiguration.openshift.io/role, operator: In, values: [worker,infra]}
48	  nodeSelector:
49	    matchLabels:
50	      node-role.kubernetes.io/infra: ""
51	```
52	
53	```console
54	$ oc create -f infra.mcp.yaml
55	```
56	
57	You can check that the custom MCP has now been created:
58	
59	```console
60	$ oc get mcp
61	NAME     CONFIG                                             UPDATED   UPDATING   DEGRADED
62	infra    rendered-infra-6db67f47c0b205c26561b1c5ab74d79b    True      False      False
63	master   rendered-master-7053d8fc3619388accc12c7759f8241a   True      False      False
64	worker   rendered-worker-6db67f47c0b205c26561b1c5ab74d79b   True      False      False
65	```
66	
67	The example above makes an `infra` pool that contains all of the MachineConfigs used by the `worker` pool.
68	
69	## Deploy changes to a custom pool (optional)
70	
71	Deploying changes to a custom pool is just a matter of creating a MachineConfig that uses the custom pool name as the label (`infra` in the example):
72	
73	```console
74	$ cat infra.mc.yaml
75	apiVersion: machineconfiguration.openshift.io/v1
76	kind: MachineConfig
77	metadata:
78	  labels:
79	    machineconfiguration.openshift.io/role: infra
80	  name: 51-infra
81	spec:
82	  config:
83	    ignition:
84	      version: 2.2.0
85	    storage:
86	      files:
87	      - contents:
88	          source: data:,infra
89	        filesystem: root
90	        mode: 0644
91	        path: /etc/infratest
92	```
93	
94	```console
95	$ oc create -f infra.mc.yaml
96	```
97	
98	The custom `infra` pool has now deployed your change with the `infra`-targeted MachineConfig:
99	
100	```console
101	$ oc get mcp
102	NAME     CONFIG                                             UPDATED   UPDATING   DEGRADED
103	infra    rendered-infra-dfdfdf7e006f18cd5d29cae03f77948b    True      False      False
104	master   rendered-master-7053d8fc3619388accc12c7759f8241a   True      False      False
105	worker   rendered-worker-6db67f47c0b205c26561b1c5ab74d79b   True      False      False
106	```
107	
108	Now check that the file landed on the `infra` node:
109	
110	```console
111	$ oc get pods -n openshift-machine-config-operator -l k8s-app=machine-config-daemon --field-selector "spec.nodeName=ip-10-0-130-218.us-west-1.compute.internal"
112	NAME                          READY   STATUS    RESTARTS   AGE
113	machine-config-daemon-vxb4c   1/1     Running   2          43m
114	```
115	
116	```console
117	$ oc rsh -n openshift-machine-config-operator machine-config-daemon-vxb4c chroot /rootfs cat /etc/infratest
118	infra
119	```
120	
121	## Removing a custom pool
122	
123	Removing a custom pool requires first to un-label each node:
124	
125	```console
126	$ oc label node ip-10-0-130-218.us-west-1.compute.internal node-role.kubernetes.io/infra-
127	```
128	
129	Note: a node must have a role at any given time to be properly functioning. If you have infra-only nodes,
130	you should first relabel the node as worker and only then proceed to unlabel it from the infra role.
131	
132	```console
133	$ oc get nodes
134	NAME                                         STATUS   ROLES    AGE   VERSION
135	ip-10-0-130-218.us-west-1.compute.internal   Ready    worker   50m   v1.14.0+e020ea5b3
136	ip-10-0-131-9.us-west-1.compute.internal     Ready    master   56m   v1.14.0+e020ea5b3
137	ip-10-0-134-237.us-west-1.compute.internal   Ready    master   56m   v1.14.0+e020ea5b3
138	ip-10-0-138-167.us-west-1.compute.internal   Ready    worker   50m   v1.14.0+e020ea5b3
139	ip-10-0-151-146.us-west-1.compute.internal   Ready    master   56m   v1.14.0+e020ea5b3
140	ip-10-0-152-59.us-west-1.compute.internal    Ready    worker   50m   v1.14.0+e020ea5b3
141	```
142	
143	The MCO is then going to reconcile the node to the `worker` pool configuration.
144	
145	```console
146	$ oc get mcp
147	NAME     CONFIG                                             UPDATED   UPDATING   DEGRADED
148	infra    rendered-infra-dfdfdf7e006f18cd5d29cae03f77948b    True      False      False
149	master   rendered-master-7053d8fc3619388accc12c7759f8241a   True      False      False
150	worker   rendered-worker-6db67f47c0b205c26561b1c5ab74d79b   False     True       False
151	```
152	
153	As soon as the `worker` pool reconciles, you can remove the `infra` MCP and any MC created.
154	
155	```console
156	$ oc get mcp
157	NAME     CONFIG                                             UPDATED   UPDATING   DEGRADED
158	infra    rendered-infra-dfdfdf7e006f18cd5d29cae03f77948b    True      False      False
159	master   rendered-master-7053d8fc3619388accc12c7759f8241a   True      False      False
160	worker   rendered-worker-6db67f47c0b205c26561b1c5ab74d79b   True      False      False
161	
162	$ oc delete mc 51-infra
163	machineconfig.machineconfiguration.openshift.io "51-infra" deleted
164	
165	$ oc delete mcp infra
166	machineconfigpool.machineconfiguration.openshift.io "infra" deleted
167	```
168	
169	## Custom pool on master node
170	Custom pool on a node having master role is not supported. `oc label node` will apply the new custom role to the target master node but MCO will not apply changes specific to the custom pool. Error can be seen in the Machine Config Controller pod logs. This behaviour is to make sure that control plane nodes remain stable.
171	
172	## Understanding custom pool updates
173	
174	A node can be part of at most one pool.  The MCO will roll out updates for pools independently; for example, if there is an OS update or other change that affects all pools, normally 1 node from the `master` and `worker` pool would update at the same time.  If you add an `infra` pool for example, then 1 node from that pool will also try to roll out concurrently with the `master` and `worker`.
175

4	  name: layered-worker

7	    matchLabels:
8	      "machineconfiguration.openshift.io/role": "layered-worker"
9	  nodeSelector:
10	    matchLabels:
11	      node-role.kubernetes.io/layered-worker: ""
